text.title: GCP Microservices and Data Pipeline Patterns
text.summary: Cloud Run patterns, Pub/Sub with DLQ, Dataflow streaming, and BigQuery partitioning rules.

rule.statement: Use Cloud Run for stateless HTTP workloads; bind each Cloud Run service to a dedicated service account with minimal IAM roles; set concurrency and CPU allocation based on workload type.
rule.rationale: Per-service accounts enable auditing of which service accessed which resource. Incorrect concurrency leads to cold start storms (too low) or memory exhaustion (too high).

rule.statement: Enable VPC connector or Direct VPC egress for Cloud Run services that need to access Cloud SQL, Memorystore, or internal services; do not expose internal APIs via public Cloud Run URLs.
rule.rationale: VPC egress routes Cloud Run traffic through the VPC, enabling access to private resources without public exposure or NAT gateway costs.

rule.statement: Configure a dead-letter topic on every Pub/Sub subscription with max_delivery_attempts = 5; use exactly-once delivery for financial and idempotency-critical workflows.
rule.rationale: Without a dead-letter topic, messages that fail processing are retried indefinitely, blocking the subscription and delaying subsequent messages. DLT enables failure inspection and replay.

rule.statement: Use Push subscriptions (Cloud Run, Cloud Functions) for event-driven workloads; use Pull subscriptions for batch consumers that need to control processing rate.
rule.rationale: Push subscriptions trigger immediately on message arrival. Pull subscriptions give consumers control over throughput and prevent overloading downstream systems.

rule.statement: Partition BigQuery tables on ingestion date or a high-cardinality timestamp field; cluster on the most common filter columns; use Reservations for critical queries.
rule.rationale: Partitioning reduces bytes scanned per query by 10-100x. Clustering further reduces data scanned within partitions. On-demand pricing without reservations can create unpredictable costs for large queries.

rule.statement: Design Dataflow pipelines to be idempotent and restartable; use checkpointing for streaming pipelines; define watermarks for late-arriving data handling.
rule.rationale: Dataflow workers can fail and restart mid-job. Non-idempotent pipelines produce duplicate records on restart. Watermarks define when a window is considered complete.

antipattern.description: Pub/Sub subscription without a dead-letter topic for production workloads.
antipattern.reason: A single bad message can block an entire subscription if there is no DLT to absorb it after max retries. The subscription falls arbitrarily behind with no alerting mechanism.
